label: MetLife Stadium — FINAL
sub: Home of the 2026 World Cup Final (July 19). Capacity 82,500. Located in East Rutherford, NJ — the largest World Cup Final venue since Brazil's Maracanã in 2014.

label: AT&T Stadium — Dallas
sub: Hosts 9 matches — more than any other venue. Semi-final host. Capacity 70,122. Located in Arlington, TX. Features a retractable roof and the world's largest column-free interior.

label: Mercedes-Benz Stadium — Atlanta
sub: Semi-final host. Capacity 67,382. Features an innovative oculus retractable roof. Home of Atlanta United MLS club and the Atlanta Falcons NFL team.

label: SoFi Stadium — Los Angeles
sub: USA opening match venue (June 12). Capacity 70,000. Built in 2020 at a cost of $5.5 billion — the most expensive stadium ever built. Also hosts the 2028 Olympic ceremonies.

label: NRG Stadium — Houston
sub: Capacity 68,311. Home of the Houston Texans NFL team. Features a retractable roof — critical in Houston's humid summer climate. One of the tournament's central-region venues.

label: Levi's Stadium — San Francisco Bay Area
sub: Capacity ~68,500. Located in Santa Clara, CA. Home of the San Francisco 49ers. Known for innovative solar panels and environmental sustainability features.

label: Hard Rock Stadium — Miami
sub: Capacity ~65,000. Located in Miami Gardens, FL. Home of the Miami Dolphins NFL team. Florida's tropical climate and large Latin American fanbase make this a culturally unique venue.

label: Gillette Stadium — Boston
sub: Capacity 63,815. Located in Foxborough, MA. Home of the New England Patriots NFL team. One of the most storied sports venues in the northeastern US.

label: Lincoln Financial Field — Philadelphia
sub: Capacity ~69,700. Home of the Philadelphia Eagles NFL team. Located in Philadelphia — 'The City of Brotherly Love' — a region with a passionate soccer following.

label: Arrowhead Stadium — Kansas City
sub: Capacity 67,513. Home of the Kansas City Chiefs NFL team. Consistently ranked one of the loudest stadiums in the world — a significant advantage for home crowd atmosphere.

label: Lumen Field — Seattle
sub: Capacity ~69,000. Home of the Seattle Sounders MLS team and Seattle Seahawks NFL team. Known as one of the loudest outdoor stadiums in the world. Located in the Pacific Northwest cluster.

label: Estadio Azteca — Mexico City
sub: Opening match venue (June 11: Mexico vs. South Africa). Capacity 72,766. The world's only stadium to host two World Cup finals (1970, 1986). Underwent major renovations for 2026. Third time hosting a World Cup.

label: Estadio Akron — Guadalajara
sub: Capacity 44,330. Located in Zapopan, Jalisco. Home of Club Deportivo Guadalajara (Chivas). Also hosts intercontinental playoff matches in March 2026. Second-smallest venue in the tournament.

label: Estadio BBVA — Monterrey
sub: Capacity 50,113. Located in Guadalupe, Nuevo León. Home of CF Monterrey. Hosts intercontinental playoff finals in March 2026. Monterrey was also a 1986 World Cup host city.

label: BMO Field — Toronto
sub: Canada's opening match venue. Capacity expanded to ~45,000 for the tournament. Home of Toronto FC MLS club. First men's World Cup match ever played in Canada.

label: BC Place — Vancouver
sub: Capacity ~54,500. Home of the Vancouver Whitecaps MLS club and the BC Lions CFL team. Previously hosted Opening and Closing Ceremonies for the 2010 Winter Olympics. Features a retractable roof.

label: Kennedy Center — Draw Venue
sub: Site of the FIFA World Cup 2026 Final Draw on December 5, 2025. Located in Washington, D.C. Originally planned for Las Vegas; moved to D.C. reportedly at President Trump's request.

label: FIFA World Cup 26 Headquarters — Miami
sub: FIFA's primary operational headquarters for the 2026 World Cup is located in Miami, Florida — a central hub given the city's international connectivity and large Latin American diaspora.

label: CONCACAF Headquarters — Miami
sub: CONCACAF's Miami headquarters coordinates qualification, logistics, and regional operations for the 2026 World Cup. Also served as the venue management center for the March 2026 intercontinental qualifying playoffs held in Mexico.

label: Chicago — US Soccer Training Hub
sub: Chicago serves as a key base for US Soccer Federation operations and national team training camps. The city's large soccer infrastructure and Soldier Field proximity make it a logistics center for the 2026 preparations.

label: Scotiabank Saddledome — Calgary
sub: FIFA World Cup Trophy Tour by Coca-Cola stop (April 12, 2026). Second Canadian city on the tour following Vancouver (April 9–11). The original Jules Rimet-era World Cup Trophy is on display ahead of the tournament; next stop Winnipeg (April 13). Part of FIFA's 'Canada Celebrates' initiative.

label: Kansas City Police — 911 Translation Service
sub: Kansas City PD launched real-time 911 translation enabling emergency dispatchers to communicate with non-English-speaking World Cup visitors. Part of KC's host-city preparedness initiative ahead of matches at Arrowhead Stadium.

label: Vancouver Convention Centre — FIFA Congress
sub: Site of the 76th FIFA Congress on April 30, 2026. Delegates from all 211 FIFA member nations convene to vote on the 2031 Women's World Cup host (USA/Mexico/Costa Rica/Jamaica joint bid) and address Iran's World Cup participation status. Adjacent to BC Place — one of Canada's two World Cup host venues.

label: Kino Sports Complex — Iran Training Base
sub: Confirmed as Iran's World Cup training base by the Iran Football Federation. Iran's squad plans to arrive by June 10 ahead of Group G matches at SoFi Stadium (LA) and Lumen Field (Seattle). Confirmation came despite Iran government's stated opposition to US participation.
